reasoning: As of May 2025, there are fewer than 75 human cases of H5N1 reported in the US. Getting to 10,000 cases by January 1, 2026, would require sustained, efficient human-to-human (H2H) transmission to begin almost immediately and result in an explosive, CDC-recognized outbreak. While a pandemic is theoretically possible, historical base rates of novel flu pandemics suggest the risk of one starting and reaching 10,000 confirmed cases within the next 7 months is exceptionally low (below 2%). The current risk assessment by the CDC remains 'low' with no evidence of H2H spread. Thus, this resolves YES only in the event of an imminent, rapid pandemic.
